statement: Ollama's design premise is that running LLMs locally should be as simple as running a Docker container: one command to pull, one command to run, no API keys, no cloud dependency. The entire UX is modeled on the Docker CLI (ollama pull, ollama run, ollama ps) deliberately so that developers already comfortable with containers face zero conceptual overhead. This means Ollama optimizes for ease of first use and local privacy over raw performance — it is not a production inference server and does not claim to be one.

The Modelfile is Ollama's primary configuration primitive: a Dockerfile-equivalent text file that specifies the base model (FROM), system prompt (SYSTEM), inference parameters (PARAMETER), and optional prompt template (TEMPLATE). Creating a custom Modelfile and running 'ollama create' is the correct way to persist a system prompt or parameter override — editing parameters at runtime via the API applies only to that session and is lost on restart. The Modelfile is to Ollama what docker-compose.yml is to containers: the artifact that should be version-controlled.

Ollama defaults to a context window of 2048 tokens regardless of the model's actual maximum supported context. If a conversation or prompt exceeds 2048 tokens, Ollama silently truncates from the left — the oldest messages are dropped without any warning, error, or indication in the API response. This is the most common source of 'the model forgot earlier context' complaints. The fix is to explicitly set num_ctx in the Modelfile or in the API request options, matching or approaching the model's true maximum (e.g., 131072 for Llama 3.1).

Ollama does not auto-detect and set num_ctx based on the model's advertised maximum context length. Even when pulling a model whose model card states '128K context', Ollama will still use its 2048-token default until the user explicitly sets num_ctx. Increasing num_ctx has a direct VRAM cost: a context of 128K tokens requires approximately 16GB of additional VRAM beyond the model weights for the KV cache, depending on the number of layers and heads. Setting num_ctx to the model's maximum without accounting for available VRAM causes Ollama to offload KV cache layers to CPU RAM, which reduces throughput by 10-50x.

Ollama stores and runs models exclusively in GGUF format (GPT-Generated Unified Format), the successor to GGML. GGUF files encode model weights, tokenizer, and metadata in a single binary. Quantization level is the primary tradeoff between model quality and memory footprint: Q4_K_M is the community-standard choice for balanced quality/size (roughly 50% of fp16 size with minimal perplexity increase), Q2_K halves size again with noticeable quality degradation, and Q8_0 is nearly lossless but offers little compression over fp16. The quantization suffix is part of the model tag on Ollama's registry (e.g., llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M).

Ollama keeps a loaded model in GPU memory until it has been idle for 5 minutes (configurable via OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE), then unloads it to free VRAM. When a second model is requested while the first is still loaded, Ollama unloads the first immediately if there is insufficient VRAM for both. This lazy-unload strategy means the first request to a recently-evicted model incurs a 5-30 second cold-load penalty. In multi-model workflows, setting OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=0 (unload immediately after each request) or OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1 (never unload) are the two production-relevant extremes; the 5-minute default is designed for interactive exploration, not automation.

Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST API at /v1/chat/completions and /v1/completions, making it a drop-in replacement for the OpenAI SDK in local development. Setting base_url='http://localhost:11434/v1' and api_key='ollama' (any non-empty string) in the OpenAI Python client routes all calls to the local Ollama instance. The compatibility layer covers the core chat completions and embeddings endpoints but does not implement assistants, files, fine-tuning, or the batch API. Function calling support depends on whether the specific model's GGUF was packaged with a tool-use prompt template; models without one will attempt function calling but produce malformed JSON tool calls.

Ollama's concurrent request handling is controlled by OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL (default 1 on CPU, up to 4 on GPU). With the default of 1, all requests are serialized — a second request blocks until the first completes. This is appropriate for interactive single-user use but causes severe queuing in multi-user or API gateway scenarios where even 2-3 concurrent users experience long waits. Increasing OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL beyond the GPU's parallel capacity causes context fragmentation: each concurrent slot shares the same total KV cache budget, so 4 parallel slots at num_ctx=8192 each consume the same VRAM as a single slot at num_ctx=32768. The correct formula is: OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL * num_ctx must fit in available VRAM KV cache budget, or slots will spill to CPU RAM and negate the parallelism gain.

Ollama's embedding endpoint (/api/embeddings or the OpenAI-compatible /v1/embeddings) runs the model in embedding mode, which is architecturally different from generation mode: it does not use the generation KV cache and processes the entire input in a single forward pass. The practical implication is that embedding requests are significantly cheaper than generation requests in terms of GPU time — a 512-token embedding takes roughly the same compute as generating 5-10 tokens. Ollama supports using the same model file for both generation and embeddings, but dedicated embedding models (nomic-embed-text, mxbai-embed-large) are 5-10x faster and produce higher quality dense vectors than generalist models used in embedding mode. The Ollama model library's 'embedding' tag filters to purpose-built embedding models.

Ollama's GPU layer offloading (num_gpu parameter in the API or OLLAMA_NUM_GPU env var) controls how many model layers run on GPU vs CPU. Setting num_gpu=0 forces full CPU inference even on a GPU machine — this silently works but is orders of magnitude slower. Setting num_gpu higher than the actual layer count is treated as 'all layers', which is safe. The failure mode specific to multi-GPU systems: Ollama does not automatically distribute layers across multiple GPUs; it uses only the GPU with the most free VRAM unless OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD=true is set. On a 4-GPU server where only one GPU has the model loaded, the other three GPUs sit idle while the first is overloaded. OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD distributes layers evenly across all detected GPUs, enabling utilization of aggregate VRAM for models that exceed single-GPU capacity.

Ollama's REST API has two distinct generation endpoints with different streaming behaviors: /api/generate (raw completion) and /api/chat (chat with message history). The critical behavioral difference: /api/generate accepts a single 'prompt' string and manages context internally using the 'context' token array returned in each response; /api/chat accepts a messages array in OpenAI format and the server reconstructs the full conversation on each call. Using /api/generate and manually tracking the 'context' array is more token-efficient for multi-turn conversations because Ollama reuses the cached KV state from the previous turn — this is Ollama's version of prefix caching. Using /api/chat requires re-encoding the full message history on every turn. For high-frequency conversational use cases on resource-constrained hardware, /api/generate with context tracking can reduce per-turn latency by 30-60% for long conversations.

Ollama does not support fine-tuning — it is a pure inference runtime. The Modelfile ADAPTER command allows loading LoRA adapters (in GGUF format) on top of a base model, but the adapter weights must be pre-trained externally using tools like llama.cpp's finetune, axolotl, or Unsloth. The workflow is: train the adapter externally → convert to GGUF format → reference in Modelfile via ADAPTER → build with ollama create. A common misconception is that Ollama's 'create' command trains a model — it only packages an existing model with configuration. Attempting to use Ollama as a training framework wastes significant debugging time. For local fine-tuning, Unsloth with QLoRA is the current best-practice choice for consumer hardware, producing GGUF adapters that are directly compatible with Ollama.

Ollama's default listening address (0.0.0.0:11434 when OLLAMA_HOST is set to 0.0.0.0) exposes the API on all network interfaces with no authentication. Any process or device on the same network can pull models, run inference, and consume GPU resources. On cloud VMs with public IPs, this results in cryptominer-style abuse where external actors run inference workloads on the exposed Ollama instance. The default localhost-only binding (127.0.0.1:11434) is safe; only change it when there is a specific need for remote access, and in that case always put Ollama behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) with authentication middleware. The Ollama project has explicitly stated there is no built-in authentication and recommends network-layer controls. Running Ollama on a public cloud instance without a firewall rule restricting port 11434 is a common and costly mistake.

Ollama stores model weights in ~/.ollama/models/ (Linux/macOS) or %USERPROFILE%\.ollama\models\ (Windows) as a content-addressed blob store where each file is named by its SHA256 hash. The manifest file at models/manifests/ maps model names and tags to their constituent blob hashes. This architecture means: (1) two models sharing identical layers (e.g., two quantizations from the same base) share the underlying blobs, saving disk space; (2) ollama rm removes the manifest but does not garbage-collect blobs that are referenced by other models; (3) disk usage can be misleading — 'ollama list' shows per-model sizes, but actual disk usage is deduplicated. Running 'ollama rm' on all models does not free space until the orphaned blobs are cleaned manually. There is no built-in garbage collection command; orphaned blobs must be deleted by comparing the manifests directory against the blobs directory.

Ollama's mmap (memory-mapped file) model loading is its default strategy on macOS and Linux: model weights are mapped into virtual memory but not loaded into RAM or VRAM until actually accessed. On a cold start, the first inference request triggers page faults that load the needed weight pages from disk, producing the characteristic 10-60 second 'cold load' delay. Subsequent requests reuse the mapped pages from OS file cache, which is fast. The implication for deployment: if the OS is under memory pressure and evicts Ollama's file cache pages, the next request re-incurs the cold load latency. Setting OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1 keeps the model process alive but does not prevent OS page eviction. On systems with insufficient RAM to hold model weights in file cache, request latency will be inconsistent. The only reliable solution is to ensure system RAM >= model file size to keep the full file cache hot.

Ollama bundles model weights, runtime, and a local REST API server into a single binary to eliminate complex GPU driver setup and Python environment management. Unlike llama.cpp or vLLM which require manual model download and configuration, Ollama handles model storage in a standardized registry format (~/.ollama/models), automatic quantization selection based on available VRAM, and serves a persistent OpenAI-compatible endpoint on port 11434. The single binary design means local LLM inference requires only ollama serve and ollama run <model>, reducing developer setup from hours to minutes. This bundled approach trades fine-grained control over runtime parameters for dramatic simplicity.

Ollama's VRAM auto-selection picks the quantization level that fits in available VRAM without explicit user configuration. However, on systems with both integrated and discrete GPUs, Ollama may incorrectly identify integrated GPU VRAM as available and attempt to load large models into shared system memory, causing swap thrashing and severe performance degradation. The OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD environment variable and explicit GPU selection via CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES are needed to prevent integrated GPU selection. Symptoms include model load times over 60 seconds and inference speeds under 1 token/second despite an adequate discrete GPU being present.

Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST API at /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, and /v1/embeddings endpoints. Any application using the OpenAI Python SDK can be redirected to a local Ollama instance by setting base_url=http://localhost:11434/v1 and api_key=ollama (any non-empty string). This compatibility enables local development and testing with free open-source models before deploying against OpenAI, with no code changes required. The Modelfile system allows customizing system prompts, temperature defaults, and context lengths for any model, effectively creating custom model configurations accessible by name.

Ollama's context window for a model is set at model load time and cannot be changed per-request without reloading the model. The default context length for most models in Ollama is 2048 tokens, even for models that support 128k context in their full configuration. To use a larger context window, create a Modelfile with PARAMETER num_ctx 32768 or pass num_ctx in the request options. Not setting num_ctx appropriately for long-document tasks causes silent truncation at 2048 tokens with no warning, where the model simply stops processing earlier context without indicating what was dropped.

To run Ollama as a persistent background service on Linux: (1) install Ollama via the official install script which creates /etc/systemd/system/ollama.service, (2) configure the service file to add environment variables: OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 to bind to all interfaces, OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4 for concurrent request handling, (3) run systemctl enable ollama && systemctl start ollama, (4) verify with systemctl status ollama. For GPU access in the systemd service, add the GPU device to the service's DeviceAllow. The OLLAMA_HOST environment variable controls the bind address; default localhost-only binding prevents access from other machines on the network.

Using Ollama with multiple models for tiered inference: configure a lightweight model (e.g., phi3-mini or qwen2.5:1.5b) for fast simple tasks and a larger model (e.g., llama3.1:70b) for complex reasoning. Ollama keeps the most recently used model in VRAM; switching models causes a model swap taking 10-30 seconds. For latency-sensitive applications, minimize model switches by batching all requests of the same type together. The OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS environment variable controls how many models can be held in VRAM simultaneously on multi-GPU systems, enabling true concurrent multi-model serving without swap overhead.

Ollama models pulled from the registry are stored as blob files in ~/.ollama/models/blobs using a content-addressed storage scheme. Manually deleting blobs without using ollama rm corrupts the manifest index, causing subsequent ollama run commands to fail with a manifest not found error even though the blob files still exist. Always use ollama rm <model> for model deletion. If the manifest index is corrupted, remove the ~/.ollama/models/manifests directory and re-pull required models. The blobs directory can grow to hundreds of GB on systems with many models and must be monitored for disk space.

Building a private local RAG system with Ollama: (1) use Ollama's nomic-embed-text model for document embedding via the /api/embeddings endpoint, (2) store embeddings in ChromaDB running locally, (3) for retrieval, embed the query with the same model, (4) retrieve top-k chunks from ChromaDB, (5) pass retrieved context to llama3.1:8b via Ollama's chat API. The entire pipeline runs without internet access after initial model pulls. nomic-embed-text produces 768-dimensional embeddings compatible with most vector stores. The key advantage over cloud RAG is complete data privacy since no documents or queries leave the local machine.

Ollama's native REST API differs from the OpenAI-compatible /v1 endpoints in important ways. The native /api/generate endpoint takes a prompt string and model name; /api/chat takes a messages array. Both support stream: true which returns newline-delimited JSON chunks. The native API includes additional fields not in the OpenAI spec: eval_count (tokens evaluated), eval_duration (inference time in nanoseconds), load_duration (model load time), and prompt_eval_count (prompt token count). These timing fields enable precise performance profiling. The /api/show endpoint returns model metadata including parameter count, quantization type, and GGUF format details.

Ollama's chat template for models must match the model's training format. When pulling a community model file from Ollama's registry that uses an incorrect chat template in its Modelfile, the model produces incoherent responses because system and user message delimiters are wrong. Symptoms include the model echoing the user's message prefix, generating responses in the wrong language, or failing to follow system prompt instructions. Verify the chat template against the original model card on HuggingFace before deployment. Custom Modelfiles can override the template using the TEMPLATE directive. Models in GGUF format embed the correct template in metadata, but Modelfile TEMPLATE overrides take precedence.

Ollama uses GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) as its model storage format because GGUF encodes both model weights and metadata (tokenizer, architecture, quantization details, chat template) in a single self-contained file. Unlike earlier formats requiring separate config.json, tokenizer files, and weight shards, GGUF allows Ollama to load any supported model from a single file without network access to model hubs. GGUF also supports memory-mapped loading where the OS maps the file directly into virtual address space, allowing models larger than physical RAM to be used via demand paging (with performance degradation for layers not in RAM).

Ollama's streaming API returns a final JSON object after the stream completes with done: true and performance statistics. Code that breaks out of the streaming loop before the done: true message is received loses the performance stats and may not properly close the connection. On Windows, Ollama uses a named pipe for local IPC which has different buffering behavior from TCP sockets on Linux/macOS; streaming response chunks may be batched on Windows causing perceived higher first-token latency. Always read all chunks until done: true before processing results when using streaming mode.

Deploying Ollama in Docker for team use: (1) use ollama/ollama:latest image with --gpus all for GPU access, (2) mount ~/.ollama/models to a named volume for model persistence across container restarts, (3) set OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 in environment to allow external connections, (4) expose port 11434, (5) add a healthcheck using curl http://localhost:11434/api/version. The named volume prevents re-downloading models on each container restart, which for large models (70B+ = 40GB+) would be impractical. For multi-GPU hosts, set NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES in the container to restrict GPU access if co-hosting other GPU workloads.

Ollama's default request timeout is infinite on the server side, but clients with their own timeouts (curl --max-time, Python requests with timeout parameter) may terminate the connection before a large model finishes generating. For 70B models generating at 3-5 tokens/second, a 5000-token response takes 15-25 minutes. Client-side timeouts set to 30 seconds (a common default) silently cut off these long generations. When using Ollama in production pipelines, set client timeouts to at least 600 seconds for large models or implement streaming consumption to keep the connection alive throughout generation.

Ollama supports multi-modal models including llava and bakllava for image understanding. Images can be passed to the /api/chat endpoint as base64-encoded strings in the images array field of a message. The model receives both the text prompt and image together in a single request. For document analysis workflows, combining llava for visual page analysis with a text model for synthesis enables processing of PDFs with charts, diagrams, and mixed text-image content entirely locally. Image resolution should be resized to 1024x1024 or smaller before base64 encoding to reduce request payload size and improve inference speed.

Implementing a local code assistant with Ollama using code-specialized models: deploy deepseek-coder:6.7b or qwen2.5-coder:7b with a Modelfile setting PARAMETER num_ctx 16384 for sufficient code context. For IDE integration (VS Code, Neovim), connect via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Use separate model instances for code completion (smaller, faster model) and code explanation (larger model). The fill-in-the-middle (FIM) capability of deepseek-coder models enables inline code completion by passing prefix and suffix tokens in the prompt, matching GitHub Copilot's behavior for single-line and multi-line completions.

When Ollama is updated to a newer version, existing GGUF model files in the blobs directory remain compatible but new model pulls may use updated GGUF versions that are faster or more accurate. Applications pinning to a specific ollama model tag (e.g., llama3.1:8b) get the current default quantization at pull time, which changes as Ollama updates default quantizations. A pull on a different machine may get a different quantization than the original pull, causing subtle behavioral differences between development and production. Pin the full quantization tag (e.g., llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M) for reproducible deployments.

To create a custom Ollama model from a local GGUF file: (1) download the GGUF from HuggingFace using huggingface-cli download <repo> --include *.gguf, (2) create a Modelfile with FROM /path/to/model.gguf, add SYSTEM, TEMPLATE, and PARAMETER directives as needed, (3) run ollama create my-model -f Modelfile, (4) verify with ollama list, (5) test with ollama run my-model. The FROM directive accepts both registry model names and local file paths. Custom models appear in ollama list and can be used identically to registry-pulled models. Templates must use Go template syntax matching Ollama's built-in template functions.

Ollama's concurrent request handling is limited by OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL (default 1). With the default setting, all requests are queued and processed sequentially. For multi-user deployments where concurrent users are expected, OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL must be increased, but each parallel request requires proportionally more VRAM since KV cache is allocated per concurrent request. A 70B model using 8GB KV cache with OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4 requires an additional 32GB VRAM beyond the base model weights. Calculate VRAM requirements as: model_size + (num_ctx_tokens * num_layers * 2 * dtype_bytes * num_parallel) before increasing parallelism.

Ollama's keep_alive parameter (default 5 minutes) controls how long a model stays loaded in VRAM after the last request. The design choice to unload idle models prevents VRAM monopolization by inactive models on shared machines. However, the 5-minute default causes cold-start delays for applications with sporadic usage patterns where requests arrive more than 5 minutes apart. Setting keep_alive=-1 keeps the model loaded indefinitely (until explicitly unloaded or server restart), which is appropriate for dedicated single-model deployments. Setting keep_alive=0 unloads the model immediately after each request, reducing VRAM usage at the cost of full model reload latency for every request.

Ollama supports structured output via the format parameter in API requests. Setting format: json in the request body enables JSON mode where the model always returns valid JSON. For schema-constrained output, setting format to a JSON Schema object uses constrained decoding to enforce the schema structure, similar to OpenAI's structured outputs. This is particularly useful for extraction pipelines running against local models that have weaker instruction-following than GPT-4o, as constrained decoding guarantees schema compliance regardless of model quality. The feature requires llama.cpp's grammar-based sampling which is included in all Ollama distributions from version 0.1.9 onwards.

Ollama's macOS Metal (Apple Silicon) backend uses unified memory architecture where CPU and GPU share the same memory pool. Unlike discrete GPU systems where VRAM is separate from system RAM, Apple Silicon treats memory allocation as a single pool. When Ollama loads a 13B model requiring 8GB and other applications are using 12GB of the 16GB total, the model loads into swap, causing inference speeds under 1 token/second instead of the expected 15-20 tokens/second. Monitoring actual memory pressure via Activity Monitor (not just model size vs total RAM) is essential on Apple Silicon. Closing other memory-intensive applications before loading large models dramatically improves performance.

Using Ollama for batch document processing with rate self-limiting: implement a producer-consumer pattern where a producer enqueues document processing tasks and N consumer threads each maintain their own Ollama connection. Set N to OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL to match server concurrency. Each consumer processes one document at a time, sending requests and waiting for completion before taking the next task. This approach saturates Ollama's parallel processing capacity without overloading the request queue. For heterogeneous document sizes, use a priority queue that processes shorter documents first to reduce average wait time for quick responses.

Ollama's Python library (pip install ollama) provides a thin wrapper over the REST API with synchronous and async clients. The Client class exposes chat(), generate(), embeddings(), pull(), push(), create(), copy(), delete(), list(), and show() methods matching the REST API. The AsyncClient provides async counterparts. Both clients accept host parameter for connecting to remote Ollama instances. The streaming interface returns a generator of response objects rather than raw JSON, with the final object containing total timing statistics. Unlike the OpenAI Python SDK, the Ollama Python library does not handle retries or timeout configuration automatically.

Ollama's embedding models return different vector dimensions than expected when the model is pulled without specifying the exact variant. For example, nomic-embed-text:latest and nomic-embed-text:v1.5 produce 768-dimensional embeddings, but a future version may change this. Mixing embeddings from different model versions in the same vector store causes incorrect similarity calculations because dimensions differ or the embedding space is not aligned. Always pin the exact model version for embedding models (e.g., nomic-embed-text:v1.5) and never mix embeddings from different model versions in the same index.

For privacy-sensitive enterprise deployments, Ollama with network isolation provides a secure local LLM serving architecture. Configure Ollama to bind only to localhost (default), place it behind an internal nginx reverse proxy that handles authentication and rate limiting, and use firewall rules to block direct external access to port 11434. Add request logging at the nginx layer to capture model usage without logging message content. The isolation architecture ensures LLM requests never leave the corporate network, satisfying GDPR and HIPAA data residency requirements for AI-assisted tools handling sensitive data.

Ollama's Modelfile supports the ADAPTER directive for applying LoRA fine-tuned adapter weights on top of a base model. This enables deploying custom fine-tuned models without full model retraining by combining a standard base model from the registry with organization-specific LoRA adapters. The adapter must be in GGUF adapter format. The combined model appears as a regular Ollama model in the registry. This pattern allows maintaining a single base model while supporting multiple fine-tuned variants (one per department or use case) with much smaller per-variant storage requirements than full model copies.

Ollama's Windows support requires Windows 10 or 11 with NVIDIA CUDA 12.x or AMD ROCm drivers. CPU-only inference works on Windows without GPU drivers but is limited to 4-8 tokens/second for 7B models, making it impractical for interactive use. On Windows without a supported GPU, Ollama falls back to CPU inference without warning, and users may not realize they are running in degraded mode. The llama.cpp backend that Ollama uses on Windows does not support AVX-512 SIMD instructions as efficiently as the Linux build, so Windows CPU inference is 20-30% slower than Linux CPU inference on equivalent hardware.

Ollama's decision to use llama.cpp as its underlying inference engine rather than Transformers or TensorRT is based on llama.cpp's focus on CPU-compatible quantized inference. llama.cpp implements custom SIMD-optimized matrix multiplication routines (AVX2, NEON, Metal) that outperform PyTorch on CPU for small batch inference, and its GGUF quantization format reduces model size by 4-8x with 2-5% quality degradation versus FP16. The tradeoff is that llama.cpp lacks advanced batching features (continuous batching, PagedAttention) needed for high-throughput server deployments, making Ollama better suited for developer workstations and small team deployments than enterprise production servers.

Ollama's default system prompt (defined in the model's Modelfile) is overridden when a system message is included in the API request's messages array. However, when using the /api/generate endpoint (not /api/chat), the system parameter takes a string that replaces the Modelfile's system prompt entirely rather than being appended to it. Applications mixing the generate and chat endpoints for different call types may observe inconsistent system prompt behavior. Standardize on the /api/chat endpoint with explicit system messages for consistent prompt handling across all request types.

To benchmark Ollama model performance: (1) use the eval_duration and eval_count fields from the final streaming response object to calculate tokens/second: tokens_per_second = eval_count / (eval_duration / 1e9), (2) separately benchmark time-to-first-token (TTFT) by measuring time from request send to first chunk received, (3) test with multiple concurrent users by sending parallel requests with asyncio.gather, (4) measure prompt processing speed using prompt_eval_count and prompt_eval_duration for the prefill phase. TTFT is more important for interactive applications; throughput (tokens/second) matters more for batch processing. For 7B models on modern GPUs, expect 60-150 tokens/second single-threaded.

Ollama's pull command supports streaming model downloads with progress tracking via the API. The /api/pull endpoint returns newline-delimited JSON progress objects with status (pulling manifest, pulling layer, verifying sha256, writing manifest, success), digest (layer hash), total bytes, and completed bytes. This enables building download progress UIs for team tools that pre-pull models before they are needed. The pull operation is idempotent: re-pulling an already-downloaded model only re-verifies the manifest without re-downloading layers, making it safe to call pull in application startup to ensure model availability.

Implementing a local Ollama-based agent with tool use: use the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint with tools parameter, as Ollama's tool calling support maps OpenAI function calling format for models that support it (llama3.1, qwen2.5, mistral-nemo). The model generates tool_calls responses which the application executes, appending tool results as messages. Not all models support function calling reliably; models trained with tool-use fine-tuning (llama3.1, qwen2.5) produce well-formed JSON tool calls while base models produce malformed tool calls 30-60% of the time. Always test tool-calling reliability with the specific model before building production agentic workflows.

Ollama versions before 0.3.0 did not properly implement the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint's tool_calls response format. Applications using the OpenAI Python SDK pointing to older Ollama instances fail when parsing tool_call responses because the id field format differs from OpenAI's call_ prefix convention. Update Ollama to 0.3.0+ for reliable OpenAI SDK tool calling compatibility. Alternatively, use Ollama's native /api/chat endpoint and implement tool call parsing directly, as the native API format is stable across Ollama versions.

Setting up Ollama for team development with shared model storage: (1) run Ollama on a central server with OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 and OLLAMA_MODELS pointing to a shared NFS or CIFS mount, (2) configure developer workstations to use OLLAMA_HOST=<server-ip>:11434 via environment variable, (3) add nginx basic auth proxy for authentication, (4) configure keep_alive=-1 for models in active use. Centralizing model storage on a server with a fast NVMe array eliminates redundant model storage (each 70B model is 40GB) on developer laptops. NFS-mounted model storage with OS-level caching performs comparably to local NVMe for model loading since GGUF uses memory-mapped access.

Ollama does not support batched inference (processing multiple independent prompts simultaneously in a single forward pass). Each request is processed as a separate batch of size 1. This makes Ollama throughput-inefficient for offline batch workloads compared to vLLM or TGI which use continuous batching with PagedAttention. For batch document processing tasks that don't require real-time response, tools like vLLM can achieve 5-10x higher throughput than Ollama on the same hardware due to batching efficiency. Use Ollama for interactive single-user or small-team workloads; use vLLM or TGI for high-throughput batch inference pipelines.

To migrate from OpenAI API to Ollama for local development: (1) install Ollama and pull a compatible model: ollama pull qwen2.5:7b or llama3.2:3b for lightweight use, (2) in the application, set OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://localhost:11434/v1 and OPENAI_API_KEY=ollama environment variables, (3) update model name strings from gpt-4o-mini to the local model name, (4) run the application — no other code changes required for basic chat completions, (5) handle feature gaps: structured outputs with strict mode requires falling back to JSON mode, vision requires a multimodal model like llava. Total migration effort for a basic RAG application is typically under one hour.

Ollama's temperature parameter behavior differs from OpenAI for values above 1.0. While OpenAI clamps temperature to the range 0-2, Ollama's llama.cpp backend passes temperature directly to the sampler without clamping, allowing values above 2.0 which produce increasingly incoherent text. Applications migrating from OpenAI that test with high temperature values (1.5-1.9) on Ollama may get noticeably different generation quality because the sampler interpretation differs. Additionally, Ollama's default temperature when not specified is 0.8 rather than OpenAI's 1.0, causing slightly more conservative default behavior without explicit configuration.

Ollama's Modelfile SYSTEM directive sets a default system prompt that is prepended to all conversations using that model. This design separates model configuration (what the model is) from application configuration (what the model is doing in a specific application), analogous to Docker's CMD vs ENTRYPOINT separation. Model-level system prompts define the model's persona and capabilities; application-level system messages (passed in API requests) override or extend them. The layered approach allows creating specialized model variants (customer-support-bot, code-assistant) from the same base model while still allowing runtime prompt customization per request.

Ollama exposes model information via the /api/show endpoint and ollama show CLI command, returning the model's architecture, parameter count, quantization type, context length, embedding dimensions, and the full Modelfile including the chat template. This metadata is essential for integration validation: verifying that a model's context length matches the application's requirements, confirming the embedding dimensions before connecting to a vector store, and inspecting the chat template to ensure correct message formatting for models with non-standard prompt templates. The show endpoint works for both registry-pulled models and locally created custom models.

Ollama's ROCm support for AMD GPUs on Linux requires specific ROCm versions that match the AMD GPU architecture. Using ROCm 5.x with RDNA3 GPUs or ROCm 6.x with older GCN GPUs causes Ollama to fall back to CPU inference silently. The OLLAMA_DEBUG=1 environment variable reveals whether GPU acceleration is active in the startup logs. On AMD systems, verify ROCm installation matches the GPU with rocminfo and check that /dev/kfd and /dev/dri devices are accessible to the Ollama process. The Docker ollama/ollama:rocm image bundles the correct ROCm dependencies and eliminates compatibility issues at the cost of a larger container image.

Implementing automated model quality testing for Ollama deployments: maintain a golden test set of prompt-response pairs with expected answer characteristics, run the test set against each model after updates using a structured output classifier that scores responses on accuracy and format compliance, alert when pass rate drops below 90%. For regression testing, compare the current model's responses against a saved baseline using embedding cosine similarity (responses should be semantically similar even if not identical). This testing approach catches quality regressions from model quantization changes or Ollama version updates before they reach production users.

Ollama does not support model quantization conversion on-the-fly. To use a different quantization level than what is provided in the Ollama registry, the model must be converted externally using llama.cpp's convert_hf_to_gguf.py and quantize tools, then imported as a custom model via Modelfile. The Ollama registry provides limited quantization variants per model (typically Q4_K_M, Q8_0, and FP16 for most models). Specialized quantizations like Q2_K (for maximum size reduction) or Q6_K (for near-lossless quality) must be created from the source model and imported manually, adding significant operational overhead.

Building a local AI writing assistant with Ollama and a web UI: (1) run Ollama with mistral:7b or gemma2:9b for balanced quality and speed, (2) use Open WebUI (formerly Ollama WebUI) as the frontend, connecting via OLLAMA_BASE_URL, (3) configure Modelfiles for different writing modes (creative, academic, concise) as separate model variants, (4) enable Ollama's embedding endpoint with nomic-embed-text for document-grounded writing assistance. Open WebUI handles conversation history, model switching, and document RAG out of the box when connected to Ollama. The entire stack runs without internet connectivity after initial setup.

Ollama's context handling truncates input silently when the prompt exceeds num_ctx tokens. Unlike OpenAI which returns a 400 error for prompts exceeding the context window, Ollama truncates the beginning of the conversation history without any warning or error response. This means long conversations lose their earliest context silently, causing the model to appear to forget earlier discussed topics. Applications must implement client-side context management using a tokenizer to estimate prompt length and prune history proactively before sending to Ollama. Use the model's vocabulary tokenizer (accessible via Modelfile metadata) for accurate token counting.

Ollama's registry at ollama.com hosts community-contributed model Modelfiles rather than raw model weights. Model weights are stored as OCI-compatible layers and downloaded from CDN endpoints during ollama pull. The OCI (Open Container Initiative) layer format allows Ollama to share model layers between models (e.g., two variants of llama3 that use the same base weights share the same layer blob), reducing storage requirements. This registry architecture is why ollama pull llama3.1:8b and ollama pull llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M may download the same underlying weight blob if they share quantization artifacts.

To use Ollama for speech-to-text workflows: combine Whisper.cpp running locally with Ollama's LLM for post-processing. Pattern: (1) transcribe audio with whisper.cpp's server mode or the openai-whisper Python package, (2) pipe the transcript to Ollama for summarization, entity extraction, or action item identification. Ollama does not natively support audio input; all audio processing must be done externally before passing text to Ollama. For meeting transcription assistants, run whisper.cpp's --diarize mode to get speaker-attributed transcripts, then use Ollama to generate meeting summaries with clear speaker attributions from the diarized transcript.

Ollama 0.4+ supports running models across multiple GPUs on a single machine using tensor parallelism. Models that exceed a single GPU's VRAM are automatically split across available GPUs. For a 70B Q4 model requiring 40GB, two A100 40GB GPUs or four RTX 3090 24GB GPUs can host it. The OLLAMA_GPU_LAYERS variable (or per-request num_gpu) controls how many layers are placed on GPU vs CPU. Full GPU placement (all layers on GPU) is required for acceptable inference speed; CPU-offloaded layers run 10-50x slower than GPU layers due to memory bandwidth constraints. Monitor GPU utilization with nvidia-smi to verify balanced layer distribution across GPUs.

Using Ollama's /api/generate endpoint with raw: true bypasses the chat template formatting, sending the prompt exactly as provided to the model's tokenizer. This is only appropriate for models used in raw text completion mode. Passing a chat-formatted prompt string with raw: true to an instruction-tuned model produces poor results because the model's instruction-following capabilities depend on its specific chat template delimiters being present. Conversely, not using raw: true with a base (non-instruct) model causes the chat template to be applied to what should be a raw continuation prompt. Always match the endpoint and raw setting to the model's intended usage mode.

Implementing Ollama health monitoring for production deployments: (1) poll /api/version every 30 seconds as a lightweight health check endpoint, (2) monitor actual inference performance by tracking tokens/second from eval_duration metrics logged from real requests, (3) alert when tokens/second drops below 50% of the baseline for the model (indicating GPU throttling, thermal limits, or memory pressure), (4) monitor model load failures by watching for increased request queue depth without corresponding completion events, (5) set up automatic Ollama service restart if health checks fail for 3 consecutive polls. The /api/tags endpoint can verify all required models are loaded and available.

Ollama's /api/chat endpoint supports specifying stop sequences via the options.stop parameter, which terminates generation when any of the specified strings are encountered. This enables implementing custom response formatters that generate until a specific end marker, implement pseudo-streaming with chunk delimiters, or prevent models from generating beyond a specific section. Stop sequences are case-sensitive and matched as exact string prefixes. For models without reliable EOS token behavior (common in some fine-tuned variants), custom stop sequences provide reliable generation termination control that prevents runaway generation in production.

Using Ollama as an offline translation engine: deploy nllb-200 or madlad-400 via GGUF conversion for dedicated multilingual translation, or use llama3.1 with a translation system prompt for general-purpose translation. For document translation pipelines, split documents into paragraph-level chunks, translate each with Ollama maintaining context via the messages array (previous paragraph as assistant context), then reassemble. The paragraph-level chunking prevents context confusion in long documents while maintaining flow consistency between paragraphs. Batch multiple short segments in a single request using numbered lists to reduce per-request overhead.

Ollama's concurrent request queue has no maximum depth limit by default. On a system where model inference is slow (CPU-only, or thermal throttling), requests pile up in the queue indefinitely while clients wait. A web application using Ollama without its own request timeout will have user requests hanging for minutes. Implement a maximum queue depth at the application layer: if the Ollama request queue depth exceeds N requests (monitored via request tracking), reject new requests with a 503 error rather than queuing indefinitely. Use OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD=true to distribute load more evenly across available models in multi-model deployments.

To push a custom Ollama model to a private registry: (1) create an account at ollama.com, (2) create the model with a namespace-qualified name: ollama create myorg/mymodel:v1 -f Modelfile, (3) authenticate via ollama login, (4) push with ollama push myorg/mymodel:v1, (5) on other machines, pull with ollama pull myorg/mymodel:v1. For on-premise private registries, set OLLAMA_REGISTRY_URL to point to a self-hosted distribution server. The OCI-compatible layer format means private Ollama registries can be implemented using standard OCI distribution servers like Harbor or Docker Registry v2 with appropriate content-type handling for GGUF layers.

Ollama's Python library (ollama-python) does not support connection pooling or keep-alive connections to the Ollama server. Each API call creates a new HTTP connection. For high-frequency small requests (embedding calls for real-time search), this connection overhead adds 1-5ms per request. Use the httpx library directly with a persistent session pointing to the Ollama endpoint to maintain connection pools. Alternatively, the OpenAI Python SDK with base_url pointing to Ollama's /v1 endpoint uses httpx with connection pooling by default, making it more efficient for high-frequency request patterns.

For development workflows requiring both local testing and cloud deployment, the environment-based model switching pattern: define a MODEL_PROVIDER environment variable (ollama or openai) and a MODEL_NAME variable (llama3.1:8b or gpt-4o-mini). The application uses LiteLLM or a thin wrapper that translates between providers based on these environment variables. This enables testing locally with Ollama at zero cost, staging with a cheap cloud model, and production with the full-capability model, with only environment variable changes between environments. LiteLLM's ollama/ prefix routing handles the API format translation automatically.

Ollama's embedding API returns embeddings as a flat float64 array, but some vector databases expect float32 arrays. NumPy conversion from float64 to float32 is necessary before inserting into ChromaDB, Qdrant, or Pinecone which use 32-bit float vectors internally. Passing float64 arrays to ChromaDB causes silent type coercion that doubles memory usage; passing to Pinecone raises a type validation error. Always explicitly cast: numpy.array(embedding, dtype=numpy.float32) before vector store insertion. Additionally, Ollama's embedding endpoint does not support batch embedding of multiple texts in a single call; each text requires a separate API call, unlike OpenAI's batch embedding endpoint.

Ollama's approach of shipping a curated model registry (ollama.com/library) rather than direct HuggingFace integration shields users from the complexity of model format conversion, tokenizer configuration, and chat template setup. The Ollama team tests and packages each model with verified Modelfiles, correct chat templates, and recommended quantization levels. This curation means Ollama models work correctly out of the box but lags behind HuggingFace's model availability by weeks to months for newly released models. Applications needing day-one access to new models must convert and import manually; applications prioritizing ease of deployment benefit from the curated approach.

Ollama's think parameter, supported by reasoning models like deepseek-r1 and qwq, enables extended thinking mode where the model generates an internal reasoning chain before producing the final response. When think: true is set in the request, the API response includes a thinking field containing the reasoning process and a response field with the final answer. The thinking tokens are not counted in the response token cost but do affect generation time. For complex multi-step reasoning tasks, enabling think mode significantly improves accuracy at the cost of 3-10x longer generation time compared to standard mode.

Ollama's /api/generate endpoint does not support the images field for multimodal input; images must be sent via the /api/chat endpoint using the role: user message with an images array. Applications that attempt to pass images to /api/generate receive a 400 error. This API asymmetry is non-obvious because both endpoints appear to accept the same options. Additionally, the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint handles images differently from the native /api/chat endpoint: images must be in the message content array as type: image_url rather than in the top-level images field.

Implementing model-specific prompt optimization for Ollama: each model family has optimal prompt patterns that significantly affect output quality. For Llama models, explicit enumerated instructions work best (1. Do X. 2. Do Y.). For Mistral models, a concise directive without numbering produces better results. For Qwen models, structured role assignments with specific output format instructions work well. Maintain a model-prompt compatibility matrix in application configuration and use model_name.startswith() checks to select the appropriate prompt style. A/B test different prompt styles per model using a small golden evaluation set to quantify the quality difference before standardizing.

Ollama's num_predict parameter limits the maximum number of tokens to generate but does not guarantee the model will generate exactly num_predict tokens. The model stops at its natural EOS token or when num_predict tokens are generated, whichever comes first. Setting num_predict: -1 (default) means unlimited generation until EOS. For applications requiring bounded response length, set num_predict to a reasonable upper bound (e.g., 2048 for summaries) rather than -1 to prevent runaway generation. Unlike OpenAI's max_tokens, Ollama's num_predict does not affect billing, but it does affect response time for slow models where every token costs 100-500ms.

Local multi-agent system with Ollama: implement a coordinator agent using a larger model (llama3.1:70b) that decomposes tasks and delegates to specialist agents, each running smaller optimized models (codellama for code, llava for vision, mistral for text analysis). The coordinator communicates with specialists via the Ollama API, passing structured task descriptions and receiving structured JSON results. All agent communication happens via localhost API calls with no internet dependency. The coordinator model must reliably generate valid JSON delegation instructions; use grammar-based constrained generation (format: json) to guarantee well-formed inter-agent messages.

Ollama server logs sensitive information at the INFO level, including partial request bodies when debug mode is enabled. On shared machines where Ollama serves multiple users via a shared endpoint, enabling OLLAMA_DEBUG=1 can expose user query content to anyone with access to the server logs. Disable debug logging in production environments by ensuring OLLAMA_DEBUG is not set. For operational monitoring that requires detailed logging, use a middleware proxy layer (nginx, Caddy) to log request metadata (path, response time, status) without logging request bodies.

To run Ollama in Kubernetes for team deployments: (1) use the ollama/ollama:latest image as a Deployment with 1 replica (Ollama does not support horizontal scaling with shared state), (2) configure a PersistentVolumeClaim for /root/.ollama to persist downloaded models, (3) add NVIDIA device plugin toleration for GPU scheduling, (4) set resource limits (GPU: 1, memory: 32Gi) to prevent OOM, (5) create a ClusterIP Service on port 11434, (6) add an init container that runs ollama pull for required models before the main container starts. The init container pattern ensures models are available before the application starts without runtime download delays.

Ollama uses the GGUF quantization formats developed by the llama.cpp project because they were specifically designed to maximize inference performance on consumer hardware. Q4_K_M (4-bit mixed quantization with K-quants) provides the best quality-to-size ratio for most models: approximately 4.4 bits per weight average, with critical layers (attention, output) quantized at higher precision than feed-forward layers. This selective precision approach preserves model quality in the most sensitive architectural components while aggressively compressing the less sensitive components. Q4_K_M is the recommended default for production local deployments, achieving 95-98% of FP16 quality at 25% the memory footprint.

Ollama's automatic GPU layer selection places all model layers on GPU when VRAM is sufficient, but does not account for VRAM needed for other running GPU processes. If a PyTorch training job or another ML workload is concurrently using GPU memory, Ollama may successfully load the model during low-memory periods but fail with CUDA out of memory errors when GPU memory pressure increases from the concurrent workload. Use nvidia-smi to check available VRAM before starting Ollama model serving, and set OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD to reserve GPU memory for other processes when co-hosting multiple GPU workloads.

Using Ollama's seed parameter for reproducible generation: set options.seed to a fixed integer for deterministic output across runs. Same seed + same model + same prompt = same output (on the same hardware; hardware differences cause floating-point variations). This is valuable for testing and debugging where response variability obscures test failures, and for generating consistent examples in documentation. Combine seed with temperature: 0 for maximum determinism. Note that model updates (even minor quantization changes) break seed reproducibility since the weight values change. Version-pin the exact model quantization alongside the seed for truly reproducible generation.

Ollama's /api/copy endpoint allows duplicating an existing model under a new name without re-downloading model weights. This is useful for creating multiple Modelfile variants (different system prompts, temperatures, context lengths) from the same base model while sharing the underlying GGUF weight blob. The copy operation is near-instantaneous since only the manifest is duplicated, not the weight files. Combined with Modelfile inheritance (FROM existing-model), this enables a hierarchy of specialized models: base model -> domain-adapted variant -> use-case-specific configuration, each sharing the same underlying weight blob and only differing in their Modelfile directives.

Ollama's embedding endpoint (/api/embeddings and the OpenAI-compatible /v1/embeddings) processes one text input per request. Unlike OpenAI's embeddings API which accepts a list of strings and processes them in a single batched forward pass, Ollama processes each embedding sequentially. For indexing 10,000 documents, this means 10,000 sequential API calls. The practical solution is to use asyncio to send multiple embedding requests concurrently (up to OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL requests simultaneously), which achieves near-batch-equivalent throughput by parallelizing network I/O while the CPU/GPU processes each request.

To set up Ollama for low-memory environments (8GB RAM, no GPU): (1) select a model that fits in 4GB (phi3.5:3.8b, gemma2:2b, or qwen2.5:1.5b), (2) set OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=1 to prevent multiple models from being loaded simultaneously, (3) set num_ctx to 1024 to minimize KV cache memory usage, (4) use CPU inference by not installing GPU drivers, (5) set keep_alive=30s to free memory quickly after each use, (6) disable Ollama's GPU detection with OLLAMA_NO_GPU=1 to avoid detection overhead. CPU inference on 8GB RAM machines runs at 4-8 tokens/second for 1.5-3B models, which is usable for non-interactive batch tasks.

Ollama's /api/tags endpoint lists all locally available models but does not indicate which models are currently loaded in VRAM. A model listed by /api/tags requires a cold load (10-60 seconds for large models) if it was unloaded due to keep_alive expiry. Applications that check model availability via /api/tags before routing requests do not distinguish between warm (VRAM-loaded) and cold (disk-only) models, causing unexpected latency spikes. Use the /api/ps endpoint (available from Ollama 0.1.33+) to list currently running (VRAM-loaded) models and their memory usage, enabling routing that prefers warm models to minimize cold-start latency.

Building a local AI-powered document search engine with Ollama: (1) extract text from documents using local tools (pdfplumber, python-docx), (2) chunk into 512-token segments with 128-token overlap, (3) embed each chunk using ollama run nomic-embed-text via the embedding API, (4) store embeddings in a local SQLite database with sqlite-vec or chromadb, (5) serve search via a FastAPI endpoint that embeds the query and returns top-10 similar chunks with source context. The entire stack runs on a developer laptop with no cloud dependencies. For departments handling sensitive documents (legal, medical, HR), this architecture satisfies data residency requirements while providing modern semantic search capabilities.

Ollama chose Go as its implementation language for the server component because Go's goroutine model naturally maps to concurrent request handling, its static binary compilation simplifies cross-platform distribution (single binary for macOS, Linux, Windows), and its C interop via CGo enables direct integration with llama.cpp's C library without an FFI overhead layer. Python was considered but rejected because Python's GIL would limit concurrent request processing, and its runtime dependencies would complicate the single-binary distribution goal. The Go server handles request routing and session management while delegating the actual inference to the llama.cpp C library via CGo bindings.

Ollama does not validate that the Modelfile TEMPLATE directive uses valid Go template syntax before creating the model. An invalid template (missing closing braces, incorrect function calls) causes the model creation to succeed but all inference requests to fail with a confusing template execution error. The error only manifests at inference time, not during ollama create. Always test new Modelfile templates by running ollama run <new-model> hello immediately after creation to catch template errors before deployment. The most common template error is incorrect use of .System vs .Prompt variables which differ between the /api/generate and /api/chat endpoints.

Implementing Ollama model warming for low-latency first-response: send a dummy request (empty prompt, num_predict: 1) to load the model into VRAM during application startup before any real user requests arrive. This converts what would be a user-facing 30-60 second cold start into a pre-startup delay. Schedule model warming in an async background task that runs after the application's HTTP server is listening, so the application is responsive to health checks during warming. For multi-model deployments, warm models in priority order based on expected traffic, completing the most critical models first.

Ollama's /api/chat endpoint preserves conversation context between turns without client-side history management when using the context field returned by the server. After the first request, the server returns a context array (compressed conversation state). Passing this context in subsequent requests continues the conversation without resending all previous messages. This is unique to Ollama's native API and significantly reduces request payload size for long conversations. The context is model-specific and not transferable between models. Note: the context approach is not available via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, which requires full message history in every request.

Ollama's network binding security defaults changed between versions. Before version 0.1.24, Ollama bound to 0.0.0.0 by default, exposing the API to all network interfaces without authentication. After 0.1.24, the default changed to localhost-only. Installations upgraded from older versions may retain old configuration or have OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 set explicitly, leaving the API exposed without authentication. Run netstat -tlnp | grep 11434 to verify Ollama's actual binding address. For team deployments requiring network access, place Ollama behind an authenticated reverse proxy rather than exposing it directly with OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0.

To use Ollama with LangChain: install langchain-ollama and use OllamaLLM or ChatOllama classes. (1) ChatOllama(model=llama3.1:8b, base_url=http://localhost:11434) for chat-format models, (2) OllamaEmbeddings(model=nomic-embed-text) for embedding workflows, (3) chain with LangChain's standard LCEL composition using | operators. The base_url parameter allows pointing to remote Ollama instances. LangChain's callback system automatically logs Ollama token usage and request metadata. Unlike direct API calls, ChatOllama handles chat template formatting automatically based on the model's registered template. Streaming works via the streaming=True parameter in ChatOllama combined with LangChain's standard streaming handlers.

Ollama does not support fine-tuning models directly. Fine-tuning must be performed externally using tools like LLaMA-Factory, Axolotl, or Unsloth, and the resulting fine-tuned model must be converted to GGUF format and imported via Modelfile. The conversion pipeline: (1) fine-tune in HuggingFace format using your training framework, (2) merge LoRA adapters into the base model, (3) convert to GGUF using llama.cpp's convert_hf_to_gguf.py, (4) quantize with llama.cpp's quantize tool, (5) create Ollama Modelfile and import. This multi-step process is the only path to deploying custom fine-tuned models through Ollama.

Ollama's model loading on macOS uses mmap with MAP_PRIVATE to map GGUF files into virtual memory. On macOS 13+, large file mmap operations require the file to be on a non-encrypted APFS volume. Placing the Ollama models directory (~/.ollama/models) on an encrypted APFS volume or an external drive with different filesystem characteristics causes significantly slower model loading times (5-10x slower than expected). If Ollama model loading consistently takes over 60 seconds for a 7B model on Apple Silicon, check that the models directory is on the main internal APFS volume rather than a mounted encrypted volume or external storage.

Implementing Ollama-based content moderation for user-generated content: use a small fast model (phi3.5:mini or gemma2:2b) with a structured output format that classifies content on dimensions: harmful, spam, off-topic, with confidence scores. The lightweight model adds 200-500ms moderation latency at 5-20x lower compute cost than a 70B model. For detected violations, log the original content, model response, and classification decision for human review. The local deployment ensures moderated content never leaves the organization's infrastructure, critical for platforms handling sensitive user data. Calibrate the confidence threshold by reviewing a sample of edge cases flagged by the moderation model.

Ollama's /api/ps endpoint returns currently loaded models with their VRAM usage, KV cache size, and remaining time before keep_alive unloads them. This is the primary observability endpoint for monitoring Ollama's resource utilization. Integrating /api/ps into a monitoring dashboard enables tracking model hot/cold state, identifying unused models consuming VRAM, and alerting when VRAM pressure causes models to be unloaded unexpectedly. The size_vram field shows actual GPU memory consumption per model, allowing accurate capacity planning for multi-model deployments. Polling /api/ps every 10 seconds provides adequate monitoring granularity without significant overhead.

Ollama's behavior when the requested model is not found locally differs from OpenAI's 404 response. Instead of returning an HTTP error, Ollama either automatically pulls the model from the registry (if OLLAMA_AUTO_PULL is enabled) or returns an error with model not found in the response body with HTTP 404. Applications that check for HTTP 200 as the success indicator and then parse the body may not notice the model not found error embedded in an otherwise well-structured response. When OLLAMA_AUTO_PULL causes automatic downloads in production, a simple typo in a model name can trigger a 40GB download unexpectedly. Disable auto-pull in production and always validate model availability at startup.

Ollama-based data extraction pipeline for financial documents: (1) run qwen2.5:14b with structured output format for reliable JSON extraction, (2) process each document page independently with a page-level context to prevent cross-page confusion, (3) aggregate per-page extractions into a document-level record, (4) run a validation pass using a second model call that checks internal consistency of extracted data (e.g., sum of line items equals total), (5) flag inconsistent extractions for human review. Running at 5-10 pages/minute on a single RTX 4090, this pipeline processes a 200-page annual report in 20-40 minutes entirely locally, making it viable for organizations processing sensitive financial documents that cannot use cloud AI services.

Ollama implements its own model registry format instead of using HuggingFace Hub directly because HuggingFace models in safetensors/PyTorch format require Python and extensive dependencies to load, defeating Ollama's single-binary philosophy. The OCI-based registry format allows Ollama to distribute GGUF models as container layers using existing CDN infrastructure (same technology as Docker image distribution), enabling fast global model distribution without building new infrastructure. The SHA256 content-addressed layer deduplication means multiple model variants sharing the same base weights only download the unique layers, reducing bandwidth for multi-variant deployments.

Ollama's JSON output mode (format: json) enables constrained decoding to valid JSON but does not guarantee the JSON contains any particular keys or structure. The model must be prompted explicitly to include specific fields. Without clear prompting, models in JSON mode often generate minimal JSON objects (empty {} or {result: ...}) that don't match the expected schema. For structured data extraction requiring specific fields, always include the desired schema in the prompt (e.g., return JSON with keys: name, date, amount) and validate the response against the expected schema before using the data, as constrained decoding only guarantees JSON validity not schema compliance.

To optimize Ollama for high-throughput batch processing: (1) set OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL to 4-8 based on available VRAM (add 2GB VRAM per parallel slot for 7B models), (2) use asyncio.gather with num_parallel concurrent requests in the client, (3) disable keep_alive between batches to free VRAM: send a request with keep_alive: 0 after each batch completes, (4) monitor GPU utilization with nvidia-smi; aim for 95%+ GPU utilization during processing, (5) use a model with minimal KV cache requirements: reduce num_ctx to the minimum needed for the batch task. For 100% GPU-bound tasks, throughput scales linearly with GPU count when using multi-GPU Ollama configurations.

Ollama's OLLAMA_MODELS directory path must not contain spaces or special characters on Windows due to a Go path handling bug that was fixed in some but not all versions. Models stored in paths like C:\Users\John Smith\ollama\models cause manifest loading failures with cryptic path parsing errors on Windows. Always configure OLLAMA_MODELS to a path without spaces on Windows (e.g., C:\ollama\models). On macOS and Linux, spaces in paths are handled correctly. This limitation affects users who installed Ollama with the default user home directory when the Windows username contains spaces.

Using Ollama for continuous learning feedback loops in small teams: collect user corrections (cases where users edited AI-generated content), format them as instruction-output pairs, use them to fine-tune a small model externally (e.g., with Unsloth on Colab), convert the fine-tuned model to GGUF, and replace the Ollama model with the improved version. This feedback collection and model update cycle runs monthly for small datasets (50-200 corrections). The pattern enables gradual quality improvement tailored to the team's specific vocabulary, style, and domain without cloud fine-tuning costs. Monthly model updates keep the improvement cycle fast enough to show visible quality gains within the first quarter.

Ollama's mirostat sampling algorithm (options.mirostat=1 or 2) provides perplexity-controlled text generation as an alternative to temperature-based sampling. Mirostat dynamically adjusts the sampling temperature to maintain a target perplexity level (options.mirostat_tau), producing more consistent text quality across long generations. For long-form creative writing or technical documentation where temperature-based generation can drift to incoherence over hundreds of tokens, mirostat maintains more consistent quality. Mirostat 2 (v2) is the more stable variant and is recommended over mirostat 1. It is particularly effective for models with strong writing capabilities like Mistral and Llama variants.

When running Ollama behind a reverse proxy like nginx, response streaming breaks if the proxy buffers responses. nginx's proxy_buffering on configuration buffers the entire response before forwarding, converting streaming responses to non-streaming from the client's perspective. Set proxy_buffering off and proxy_read_timeout 600 in nginx for the Ollama upstream. For Traefik, disable response buffering with the traefik.http.middlewares.ollama.buffering.maxRequestBodyBytes=0 label. Without these settings, users see no streaming output until the full generation completes, defeating the purpose of streaming and causing timeouts for long generations.

Setting up Ollama as a local AI backend for Cursor/VS Code with Continue plugin: (1) install the Continue extension, (2) configure config.json to use Ollama with model: llama3.1:8b or deepseek-coder:6.7b for code completion, (3) set ollama_base_url to http://localhost:11434, (4) configure a larger context model (qwen2.5-coder:32b) for the chat feature if VRAM allows, (5) set num_ctx: 8192 for adequate code context. The split between a small fast model for completions (needs <200ms response) and a larger model for chat (tolerates 2-5s response) optimizes the user experience within local hardware constraints.

Ollama's model creation (ollama create) does not support conditional logic or inheritance chains longer than one level in Modelfiles. A Modelfile cannot reference another custom model as its base and also add an ADAPTER directive simultaneously — only one of FROM (existing model) + no ADAPTER, or FROM (GGUF file) + ADAPTER (GGUF adapter) is supported. Complex model composition requiring multiple LoRA adapters stacked on a base must be done externally by merging adapters before conversion to GGUF. The flat composition model keeps Modelfiles simple but limits multi-adapter deployment patterns.

Ollama's behavior when processing requests faster than the client reads the streaming output causes internal buffer overflow issues in versions before 0.3.5. If a client reads streaming chunks slowly (or processes each chunk before reading the next), Ollama's internal write buffer fills up and the server stalls, causing other concurrent requests to queue up. Always consume Ollama streaming responses as fast as possible in a background thread/coroutine, buffering received tokens for processing rather than processing inline during the read loop. This backpressure issue was fixed in 0.3.5+ but remains relevant for older deployments.

Implementing dynamic model selection in Ollama applications based on query complexity: classify queries into three tiers using a rule-based classifier (no API call needed): (1) simple: single-fact questions, greetings, yes/no — route to phi3.5:mini (fast, cheap compute), (2) medium: multi-step questions, summarization — route to llama3.1:8b, (3) complex: analysis, coding, multi-document reasoning — route to qwen2.5:32b or llama3.1:70b. The rule-based tier classifier uses query length, keyword patterns, and question type detection with 90%+ routing accuracy for most use cases without any additional LLM call overhead.

To implement Ollama model version management for teams: (1) maintain a models.json config file listing required models and their exact quantization tags, (2) create an ensure_models.sh script that reads models.json and runs ollama pull for each model if not already present, (3) add this script to team onboarding docs and CI/CD setup scripts, (4) version the models.json alongside the application code so model requirements are tracked with feature changes, (5) for model updates, update models.json with the new tag, add migration notes for breaking behavior changes. This config-as-code approach ensures all team members and deployment environments use exactly the same model versions.

Ollama's default sampler parameters (temperature 0.8, top_p 0.9, top_k 40) are set for general conversational use and may produce suboptimal results for specialized tasks. For deterministic extraction tasks, temperature must be explicitly set to 0 in each request; Ollama does not provide a way to globally override defaults without creating a custom Modelfile for every model. Applications that forget to set temperature: 0 in structured extraction requests get non-deterministic results where the same input produces different JSON outputs. Always explicitly specify all sampling parameters in production request payloads rather than relying on Modelfile or server defaults.

Ollama supports the OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1 environment variable to enable Flash Attention 2 for compatible GPUs (NVIDIA Ampere and newer, AMD RDNA2 and newer). Flash Attention 2 reduces KV cache memory usage by recomputing attention scores in blocks rather than materializing the full attention matrix, enabling longer context windows with the same VRAM. For a 7B model at num_ctx=32768, Flash Attention reduces VRAM usage by 30-40% compared to standard attention, allowing context lengths that would otherwise require more GPU memory. Enabling Flash Attention has minimal performance impact on short contexts but becomes critical for long-context RAG applications.

Ollama's decision to maintain compatibility with llama.cpp's quantization formats rather than developing its own ensures that the large ecosystem of pre-quantized GGUF models from the community (TheBloke, bartowski, and others on HuggingFace) can be imported directly. This approach allows Ollama to leverage community quantization work without duplicating the effort, and ensures Ollama benefits from llama.cpp's continuous quantization improvements (new formats like IQ2_XS, Q4_K_S) automatically when updating its bundled llama.cpp version. The tight coupling with llama.cpp means Ollama version updates often include significant performance improvements from upstream llama.cpp optimization work.

Ollama cannot run two different quantizations of the same base model simultaneously in VRAM. Both a Q4_K_M and Q8_0 version of llama3.1:8b would need to be fully loaded separately, each occupying their respective VRAM allocations. Unlike some serving frameworks that support multiple quantization tiers with dynamic routing, Ollama treats each quantization as a completely separate model. For applications requiring both fast Q4 responses and high-quality Q8 responses, the VRAM overhead is additive: a system needing both on a single machine must have sufficient VRAM for both models simultaneously.

Running Ollama inside a Docker container on macOS does not give the container access to the Apple Silicon GPU (Metal). Docker Desktop for Mac uses a Linux VM for containers, and Metal GPU access is not passed through to the Linux VM. All model inference inside Docker on macOS runs on CPU only, regardless of the --gpus flag which has no effect on macOS. For GPU-accelerated Ollama on macOS, run Ollama natively as a macOS application, not in Docker. This is a fundamental limitation of Docker Desktop's hypervisor architecture on macOS and not an Ollama issue.

Building an Ollama-based AI notebook for research using Jupyter: (1) run Ollama locally, (2) use the Python ollama library in notebook cells for LLM calls, (3) integrate with pandas for data analysis workflows where the model interprets tabular data passed as markdown tables, (4) use matplotlib for visualization with AI-generated chart descriptions, (5) cache LLM responses using functools.lru_cache keyed on the prompt hash to avoid re-running expensive model calls during notebook re-execution. The LRU cache dramatically improves iteration speed in exploratory notebooks where cells are repeatedly re-run during development. Store cache to disk using joblib.Memory for persistence across kernel restarts.

To set up Ollama model access control in a team environment: (1) run Ollama on a server with OLLAMA_HOST=127.0.0.1 (localhost only), (2) configure nginx as an authenticated reverse proxy with basic auth or JWT validation, (3) create nginx location blocks that restrict specific model endpoints to specific user groups (e.g., only ML engineers can access the 70B model endpoint), (4) log all requests with user identity for audit. The nginx filter can implement model-level access control by inspecting the model field in request bodies for POST /api/chat. This pattern provides team-level access control without requiring changes to Ollama itself.

Ollama's native API exposes the full llama.cpp sampler chain configuration through the options object, including advanced parameters not available in the OpenAI-compatible endpoint: mirostat, mirostat_tau, mirostat_eta, tfs_z (tail-free sampling), typical_p, repeat_last_n, repeat_penalty, penalize_newline, and presence_penalty. These fine-grained sampler controls enable replicating specific generation behaviors from research papers or achieving stylistic effects impossible with just temperature and top_p. For most applications, the defaults are adequate; advanced samplers are primarily useful for creative writing, code generation with specific style requirements, and research into sampling strategies.
